scriptId: cs_v1_EARLY_L40_Relationship with a boss or coworker
historyId: h_v1_EARLY_L40_Relationship with a boss or coworker

memories.content: User states that their direct manager is Marco Díaz, who runs the product group; the recipient responds with "Thanks for the heads-up — that's useful to know."
A person stated a boundary of not accepting meetings on Fridays due to a recurring personal commitment; the other person responded with thanks for sharing.

One participant stated a strict maximum of 25 minutes for synchronous check-ins and that longer blocks won’t be done; the other participant acknowledged and said they would keep check-ins within the 25-minute limit.
The user stated that written follow-ups must be summarized as exactly three bullet points for the archive, and the responder confirmed they will format follow-ups accordingly.
A message stated the relationship is mostly positive with occasional fuzzy expectations; the response acknowledged this, said it’s helpful to hear, and expressed appreciation.

User asked for ready-to-use phrasings for chat, short emails, and in-person pushback. The assistant provided ready-to-use phrasings organized into three categories: Chat (instant messages/Slack), Short emails (brief, professional), and One-liners for in-person pushback. The material includes phrases addressing Friday unavailability, 25-minute time blocks, requests for the main questions or decisions, options to split sessions or move to async updates, and deliverables like a 3-bullet summary. The response also notes a willingness to tailor phrasing to be more formal or more casual.

- User requested a brief, carefully worded checklist to follow before escalating any issue.
- Response provided a brief, carefully worded checklist to follow before escalating any issue, consisting of five items:
  1) Confirm attempts and context: verify you’ve tried available fixes, reviewed relevant docs/tickets, and can briefly explain what was tried and why it didn’t resolve the problem.
  2) Gather

User scenario: A peer consistently receives public acknowledgement for work the user helped with, and the user asks how to raise the issue without sounding like a complaint. Assistant response: Provided copy-pasteable scripts and a checklist to address attribution, including private DM scripts to the peer, a short email to manager Marco requesting guidance on attribution, in-person one-liners, and a brief pre-escalation checklist (collect examples, confirm contributions, raise privately, propose fixes, and escalate with a concise note). Also offered to adapt any script to be more formal or casual.

User asked for guidance on handling a coworker who forwards private messages and misframes their point. The assistant advised not to broadly change tone, but to keep messages clear, factual, and professional, and to call out misframing privately and factually when it happens, while documenting examples and requesting corrective framing. It provided practical steps: keep messages concise and fact-focused; raise the issue privately with a short, specific example; request corrective behavior (e.g., include original context, check with me first); escalate with examples if misframing goes public or persists. It also supplied copy-pasteable scripts for private messages, a quick correction if already public, and a brief escalation to a manager, along

Three copy-pasteable templates are provided to request clear acceptance criteria from a stakeholder without sounding nitpicky. The templates cover Chat/Slack, short email, and in-person one-liner formats. Each asks to confirm the acceptance criteria for a feature/task and specifies including: the measurable success metric, the core user scenarios that must work, and any edge cases or exclusions. The response notes you can use whichever template fits the channel and tweak one small phrase to sound natural, and that a one-paragraph version or concise draft can be prepared for review.
Guidance: use tickets/issue trackers as the primary source of truth, with a brief private log only for contextual information not suitable for the public

User asked for short scripts to politely decline unpaid side work offered by coworkers. Response provided a collection of copy-pasteable scripts across formats: casual chat/DM, direct chat/DM, short email, more formal email, and in-person one-liners. The scripts aim to be factual and brief to set a boundary. Additional variants include leaving the door open for formal/paid work and responses to pressure

User sought short, professional phrases to respond to a colleague's condescending written tone. The provided response offers brief lines categorized by purpose: Clarify (neutral), Address tone privately (impact-focused), Defuse in-thread (keep focus on work), and Firmer (if repeated); it also advises selecting a suitable line and tweaking a word to sound natural.

The user asked for guidance on requesting earlier involvement in planning cycles that affect their deliverables. The response advised framing the request to reduce rework and improve predictability, offering a lightweight involvement option, and providing concrete benefit examples. It included ready-to-use scripts for: chat/DM to a planner or PM, email to a manager, a concise 1:1 ask, and a planning-meeting one-liner; a brief prep checklist (provide 1–2 concrete examples, propose a low-effort option, explain team benefits, state a time/cadence commitment, and be open to alternatives); tone and tactics (collaborative, outcome-focused, and optionally asynchronous, with a trial period if there’s pushback). It also offered to tailor a chosen script to a specific person or project name.

A user asked for small, inexpensive ways to build goodwill with cross-functional partners, and the response provided a categorized list of ideas. Categories and examples include:

- Recognition & visibility: public shout-outs, short thank-you DMs, tagging contributors.
- Help & reciprocity: 15–25 minute pairing/troubleshooting sessions, quick one-pass doc/PR reviews, share a helpful template or checklist.
- Small thoughtful gestures: neutral snacks or e-cards for a milestone, short congratulatory notes.
- Practical collaboration moves: a concise shared one-page summary, a simple shared dashboard or status channel, a 10–15 minute alignment checkpoint before major handoffs.
- Signals of respect: ask for preferred communication style/times and follow it; respect deadlines and follow through on deliverables.
- Value-add content: short, relevant 3-bullet meeting/update syntheses; quick notes with links to useful resources or past work.
- Connections & advocacy: brief one-line introductions, advocate for their work in meetings.
- Low-risk perks & boundaries: own a small recurring pain point (e.g., weekly status post); keep gestures occasional and transparent; check expense policy if reimbursing.
- Two ready-to-use one-liners for recognition (public and private) are provided.
- Guidance to pick a few ideas that fit one’s style and rotate them to keep goodwill genuine.

User asked for a brief paragraph to request more context when a ticket lands with vague details. The response provided a suggested paragraph requesting additional context including: expected vs actual behavior, concise steps to reproduce (or sample data), business/user impact, clear acceptance criteria or success metric, relevant logs/screenshots/links, and the requested priority or target date, to help estimate effort and propose next steps.
